title: Mark failed QA items as filed through MCP
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-q2q

## What to build

Expose an MCP transition that lets agents mark a failed QA item as filed after they create or update the tracker bug elsewhere. The tool should update QA To Do state and audit history without creating, updating, or deduping tracker issues itself.

## Acceptance criteria

- The MCP tool requires exact session ID, item ID, and a non-empty filed issue ID.
- Only items currently in failed status transition to failed-filed; pending, passed, skipped, and already failed-filed items are rejected without corruption.
- The transition records history with the filed issue ID and reuses existing storage transition behavior.
- The tool does not create, update, or dedupe Beads issues or .scratch files.
- Public MCP handler tests cover successful transition, missing identifier rejection, non-empty issue ID validation, and invalid status rejection.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-8rt

title: Extract .scratch failure drafts through MCP
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-q2q

## What to build

Extend failed-item extraction for .scratch tracker sessions so local markdown workflows receive the same saved QA failure evidence and a structured .scratch bug draft without creating files.

## Acceptance criteria

- The extraction response supports .scratch tracker sessions through the same canonical failureContext contract used by Beads extraction.
- The ready-to-file draft follows the documented structured .scratch bug markdown convention and remains non-mutating.
- Screenshot evidence is represented in the draft text with saved local references preserved when upload support is unavailable.
- Existing .scratch failure draft logic is reused rather than duplicating tracker-specific markdown generation.
- Public MCP handler tests cover .scratch draft output and screenshot reference preservation.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-33g

title: Extract Beads failure evidence through MCP
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-q2q

## What to build

Expose an exact failed-item extraction path for Beads-backed QA sessions. Given session ID and item ID, agents should receive the saved failure evidence as a canonical failureContext plus a deterministic ready-to-file Beads bug draft.

## Acceptance criteria

- The MCP tool rejects missing or ambiguous identifiers and fetches a single failed QA item by exact session ID and item ID.
- The response includes saved actual behavior, screenshot metadata, local screenshot references and paths when available, and no screenshot binary bytes by default.
- The canonical failureContext includes repo context, parent issue context, QA item steps, expected result, source evidence, item fingerprint, and discovered-from issue identity.
- The ready-to-file Beads draft uses bug type, needs-triage and bug labels, QA failure fingerprint metadata, and discovered-from relation metadata by reusing existing failure draft logic.
- Public MCP handler and storage-facing tests cover saved screenshot references from app storage through MCP output.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-8rt

title: List failed QA items through MCP
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-q2q

## What to build

Expose a QA To Do MCP discovery path that lists saved failed QA items from the same local storage used by the app. The response should be compact enough for agents to scan, default to unfiled failures, allow already-filed failures when requested, and explain failed versus failed-filed semantics.

## Acceptance criteria

- A new MCP tool lists failed QA items without requiring agents to inspect SQLite directly.
- The default response includes only unfiled failed items, with an option to include failed-filed items for audit or recovery.
- Each row includes session ID, item ID, repo context, parent issue context, source issue identity when available, actual behavior, screenshot count, status, and a versioned response shape.
- The tool output and tool/parameter descriptions define failed and failed-filed semantics.
- Public MCP handler tests cover default filtering, include-filed filtering, status legend output, and compact row shape.

## Blocked by

None - can start immediately

title: PRD: Expose failed QA evidence through MCP
description:
## Problem Statement

Agents working on Sandcastle/RALPH follow-up need a reliable way to discover and extract failed QA evidence from QA To Do. Today the MCP can create or import QA sessions, but it cannot query failed QA items after the user records a failure in the app. That forces agents to inspect local SQLite or infer failure context from source issues, which is brittle and can miss the exact user-provided actual behavior and screenshot evidence.

The user wants an agent-callable MCP surface that can list saved failed QA items, extract the exact saved failure evidence in a Sandcastle-compatible shape, and mark a failure as filed after the agent creates the tracker bug elsewhere.

## Solution

Add three QA failure MCP tools.

The first tool lists failed QA items that exist in local QA To Do storage. It returns compact discovery rows and explains the status semantics so agents know that failed means user evidence exists but no tracker issue is confirmed, while failed-filed means the failure has already been recorded as filed.

The second tool returns a single failed QA item with all saved user-provided failure evidence. Its stable contract is a canonical failureContext object that matches the existing Beads and .scratch failure issue drafting context. The response also includes a ready-to-file draft issue by default so Sandcastle/RALPH agents can file a consistent bug without rebuilding tracker-specific markdown themselves.

The third tool marks a failed QA item as filed after an agent has filed or updated the tracker bug. It requires exact session ID, item ID, and filed issue ID, and only transitions currently failed items to failed-filed.

## User Stories

1. As an implementation agent, I want to list failed QA items through MCP, so that I can discover actionable failures without reading the app database directly.
2. As an implementation agent, I want list results to default to unfiled failures, so that I do not accidentally process already-filed failures.
3. As an implementation agent, I want to optionally include already-filed failures, so that I can audit or recover prior failure work.
4. As an implementation agent, I want each listed failure to include session and item identifiers, so that I can fetch the exact failure deterministically.
5. As an implementation agent, I want each listed failure to include repo and parent issue context, so that I can choose the right failed QA item when multiple repos or sessions exist.
6. As an implementation agent, I want each listed failure to include source issue identity when available, so that I can trace the failure back to the source Sandcastle/RALPH work.
7. As an implementation agent, I want each listed failure to include actual behavior, so that I can select the right failure based on the user's observation.
8. As an implementation agent, I want each listed failure to include screenshot count, so that I know whether additional evidence exists before fetching details.
9. As an implementation agent, I want the list response to define failed and failed-filed, so that status semantics are discoverable from the tool output.
10. As an implementation agent, I want MCP tool descriptions to define failed and failed-filed, so that I can choose correct parameters before calling the tool.
11. As an implementation agent, I want to fetch a failed QA item by exact session ID and item ID, so that extraction is unambiguous.
12. As an implementation agent, I want the extracted item to include the exact saved actual behavior, so that the user's failure evidence is preserved.
13. As an implementation agent, I want the extracted item to include saved screenshot metadata, so that visual evidence is not dropped.
14. As an implementation agent, I want screenshot evidence to include local references, so that tracker issues can cite app-managed evidence.
15. As an implementation agent, I want screenshot evidence to include local file paths when available, so that local tooling can attach or inspect the evidence.
16. As an implementation agent, I want screenshot extraction to avoid binary bytes by default, so that MCP responses remain small and safe.
17. As a QA reviewer, I want the evidence I provided in the app to be exactly what agents receive, so that follow-up bugs reflect my observation rather than a reconstructed guess.
18. As a QA reviewer, I want screenshots copied into app storage before extraction, so that evidence survives even if original files move.
19. As a QA reviewer, I want local screenshot references preserved when tracker upload is unsupported, so that evidence is not silently discarded.
20. As a Sandcastle/RALPH agent, I want a canonical failureContext object, so that I can pass the extracted payload directly into existing Beads or .scratch failure draft flows.
21. As a Sandcastle/RALPH agent, I want failureContext to include repo context, so that generated bug reports identify the affected repository.
22. As a Sandcastle/RALPH agent, I want failureContext to include parent issue context, so that the bug remains connected to the QA session source.
23. As a Sandcastle/RALPH agent, I want failureContext to include QA item steps and expected result, so that filed bugs explain what was tested.
24. As a Sandcastle/RALPH agent, I want failureContext to include source evidence, so that filed bugs explain why the QA check existed.
25. As a Sandcastle/RALPH agent, I want failureContext to include item fingerprint, so that dedupe behavior is stable.
26. As a Sandcastle/RALPH agent, I want the extracted payload to include discovered-from issue identity, so that Beads bugs can be linked to the source child or parent issue.
27. As a Sandcastle/RALPH agent, I want a ready-to-file draft issue returned by default, so that I can create a consistent tracker bug without rebuilding issue text.
28. As a Beads user, I want the draft issue to use bug type and needs-triage plus bug labels, so that failures enter the normal triage flow.
29. As a Beads user, I want the draft issue to include a QA failure fingerprint, so that repeated extraction does not create duplicate bugs.
30. As a Beads user, I want the draft issue to include discovered-from relation metadata, so that traceability is preserved.
31. As a .scratch user, I want the draft issue to follow the documented structured bug convention, so that local markdown tracking remains parseable.
32. As a .scratch user, I want screenshots preserved in the draft issue text, so that local evidence references survive without upload support.
33. As an implementation agent, I want to mark a failed QA item as filed through MCP after I create the tracker issue, so that the QA session can progress toward archive eligibility.
34. As an implementation agent, I want mark-filed to require exact session ID and item ID, so that I cannot accidentally mutate the wrong failure.
35. As an implementation agent, I want mark-filed to require a non-empty issue ID, so that the audit trail points to the filed tracker bug.
36. As a QA reviewer, I want mark-filed to fail unless the item is currently failed, so that completed or pending QA items are not corrupted by agent mistakes.
37. As a QA reviewer, I want mark-filed to record history, so that the transition from failed to failed-filed is auditable.
38. As a QA reviewer, I want mark-filed to avoid creating tracker issues itself, so that filing remains explicit and externally controlled by the agent workflow.
39. As a maintainer, I want the new MCP tools to reuse existing failure draft logic, so that Beads and .scratch behavior stays consistent across UI and agent workflows.
40. As a maintainer, I want failed item query behavior isolated behind a small repository interface, so that MCP handlers remain thin and testable.
41. As a maintainer, I want the response schemas versioned, so that future changes can be introduced without breaking agents.
42. As a maintainer, I want the tools to reject missing or ambiguous identifiers for detail and mark-filed operations, so that side effects are deterministic.
43. As a maintainer, I want the tools to use the existing local QA To Do storage configuration, so that app and MCP see the same sessions and evidence.
44. As a maintainer, I want tests to cover MCP behavior from the public tool handler interface, so that implementation details can change safely.
45. As a maintainer, I want tests to cover saved screenshot references from storage through MCP output, so that evidence persistence regressions are caught.

## Implementation Decisions

- Build three MCP tools: failed items list, failed item get, and failed item mark-filed.
- The list tool defaults to unresolved failed items and accepts an option to include already-filed failures.
- The list tool returns compact rows, not full source evidence or screenshot arrays.
- The list tool includes a status legend in every response.
- Tool descriptions and parameter descriptions explain failed and failed-filed semantics and warn about duplicate filing risk.
- The get tool returns a versioned failed-item payload with a canonical failureContext object.
- The canonical failureContext is the stable deep module boundary for downstream Sandcastle/RALPH compatibility.
- The failureContext shape matches existing Beads and .scratch failure issue drafting contexts.
- The get tool returns saved actual behavior and saved screenshots as the source of truth for user-provided failure evidence.
- Screenshot response data includes metadata and local references, with local file paths when available.
- Screenshot response data does not include binary bytes by default.
- The get tool includes a ready-to-file draft issue by default.
- Draft issues are non-mutating and deterministic.
- Beads draft issues use bug type, needs-triage and bug labels, QA failure fingerprint metadata, and discovered-from relation metadata.
- .scratch draft issues use the documented structured bug markdown convention.
- The mark-filed tool requires exact session ID, item ID, and filed issue ID.
- The mark-filed tool only transitions items currently in failed status to failed-filed.
- The mark-filed tool records history with the filed issue ID.
- The mark-filed tool does not create, update, or dedupe tracker issues itself.
- Failed item querying should be encapsulated in a storage-facing deep module with a small interface for list, get, and mark-filed operations.
- MCP handlers should remain thin schema validation and formatting adapters over storage and draft modules.
- Existing failure issue draft modules should be reused rather than duplicating tracker-specific markdown generation.
- Existing storage transition behavior for failed-filed should be reused rather than reimplementing status updates.
- The feature supports Beads and .scratch trackers only.
- GitHub and other trackers remain out of scope.
- Response schemas should include schemaVersion and kind fields for agent compatibility.

## Testing Decisions

- Good tests should assert externally visible MCP behavior and storage state transitions, not private helper implementation details.
- MCP handler tests should cover list defaults, include-filed behavior, empty results, status legend output, and row fields.
- MCP handler tests should cover get behavior for failed and failed-filed items, including canonical failureContext, actual behavior, source evidence, screenshot references, and draft issue output.
- MCP handler tests should cover mark-filed success and rejection when the item is not currently failed.
- Storage tests should cover repository-level failed item discovery, exact failed item retrieval, persisted screenshot metadata, local references, local paths, and history after mark-filed.
- Draft compatibility tests should verify the extracted failureContext can be passed to existing Beads and .scratch draft builders.
- Schema tests should verify required inputs for each tool and invalid identifier errors.
- Regression tests should cover user-provided failure evidence round-tripping from saved storage into MCP output unchanged.
- Prior art exists in the current MCP tool handler tests for tool-facing behavior.
- Prior art exists in storage tests for failed item screenshots and failed-filed transitions.
- Prior art exists in Beads and .scratch failure issue tests for draft issue formatting and dedupe fingerprints.

## Out of Scope

- Creating or updating Beads issues inside the new extraction tools.
- Creating or updating .scratch bug files inside the new extraction tools.
- Passing, failing, editing, skipping, archiving, deleting, or restoring QA items through MCP.
- Returning screenshot binary bytes by default.
- Uploading screenshots to trackers.
- Adding GitHub, GitLab, Jira, Linear, or other tracker support.
- Changing the QA session import schema.
- Changing the desktop failure composer UX beyond what is needed to ensure evidence is saved before extraction.
- Replacing the local app as the source of truth for QA item state.

## Further Notes

- This PRD intentionally revises the earlier v1 assumption that MCP would not expose read/query APIs for app state. The user now explicitly wants agent-callable extraction of failed QA evidence.
- The key product requirement is that whatever failure evidence the user provided must be saved and returned through MCP.
- The deep module opportunity is the failed QA evidence extraction boundary: storage query, canonical failureContext projection, draft issue projection, and mark-filed transition can be tested independently from MCP transport.
- Local screenshot references may currently differ between TypeScript storage and Tauri storage. Downstream agents should treat localReference as opaque and use localPath when they need the filesystem file.

comments.author: user
comments.text:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Expose saved failed QA evidence through MCP so Sandcastle/RALPH agents can discover, extract, draft, and mark filed QA failures without reading local SQLite directly.

**Current behavior:**
QA To Do MCP can create/import QA sessions via `qa_session_create` and `run_to_qa_parent`. The app and storage layer already persist item statuses, failure notes, screenshot references, and `failed-filed` transitions, but MCP has no query surface for saved failed QA evidence after a user records a failure in the app. Agents must inspect local storage or infer context from source issues, which risks losing the exact actual behavior and screenshot evidence provided by the user.

**Desired behavior:**
Add three MCP tools for failed QA evidence:

- List failed QA items from local QA To Do storage, defaulting to unresolved `failed` items and optionally including `failed-filed` items.
- Get one failed QA item by exact session ID and item ID, returning saved user evidence plus a stable, versioned `failureContext` compatible with existing Beads and `.scratch` failure issue drafting.
- Mark a failed QA item as filed after an agent files or updates a tracker issue, requiring exact session ID, item ID, and filed issue ID, and only transitioning currently `failed` items to `failed-filed`.

The list and tool descriptions must explain that `failed` means user evidence exists but no tracker issue is confirmed, while `failed-filed` means a tracker issue has already been recorded. Responses should make duplicate filing risk clear.

**Key interfaces:**
- `src/lib/mcpServer.ts` — register the new MCP tools and describe failed/failed-filed status semantics in tool and parameter descriptions.
- `src/lib/mcpToolHandlers.ts` — add handlers for listing failed items, fetching one failed item, and marking a failure filed.
- `src/lib/qaStorage.ts` — expose repository methods needed to query failed/failed-filed items across sessions and retrieve exact item evidence without bypassing storage abstractions. Reuse existing `markFailureFiled` behavior for the filed transition.
- `src/lib/beadsFailureIssue.ts` and `src/lib/scratchFailureIssue.ts` — reuse or align with the existing failure context and draft issue shapes, including bug type, `needs-triage` + `bug` labels, QA failure fingerprint, and discovered-from metadata.
- `src/lib/*test.ts` — add tests around externally visible MCP behavior, status filtering, payload shape, screenshot references, draft issue output, and mark-filed safety.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] MCP exposes failed QA list, failed QA get, and failed QA mark-filed tools.
- [ ] List defaults to unfiled `failed` items and has an option to include `failed-filed` items.
- [ ] List rows include session ID, item ID, repo context, parent issue context, source issue identity when available, actual behavior summary, screenshot count, status, and status legend.
- [ ] Get requires exact session ID and item ID and returns a versioned payload with canonical `failureContext`.
- [ ] Get returns saved actual behavior and saved screenshot references from storage as the source of truth.
- [ ] Get returns a ready-to-file draft issue by default, using bug type, `needs-triage` and `bug` labels, QA failure fingerprint, and discovered-from metadata.
- [ ] Mark-filed requires session ID, item ID, and filed issue ID; it rejects missing IDs and non-`failed` item states.
- [ ] Mark-filed transitions only the targeted `failed` item to `failed-filed` and records the filed issue ID in history/notes.
- [ ] Tool descriptions and parameter descriptions define `failed` and `failed-filed` semantics and warn about duplicate filing risk.
- [ ] Tests cover saved screenshot references through MCP output and status persistence through the mark-filed flow.

**Out of scope:**
- Automatically filing tracker bugs from this MCP surface.
- Mutating external Beads or `.scratch` trackers during list/get.
- Replacing existing UI failure capture or screenshot attachment flows.
- Reading SQLite directly from agents instead of using repository/tool abstractions.

title: test
Package Linux app
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Produce installable Linux artifacts so the local QA To Do app can be used on Debian/Ubuntu-style systems and other Linux distributions.

User stories covered: 79, 80.

## Acceptance criteria

- Build produces a `.deb` package that installs cleanly on Debian/Ubuntu-style systems.
- Build produces an AppImage that runs without root on other Linux distributions.
- Packaging tests cover installer dry-run output, no silent config overwrite, provider capability reporting, and safe fallback instructions.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.1
- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.12

title: Install OpenCode /to-qa provider workflow
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Provide an AFK-safe OpenCode setup path for the global `/to-qa` skill and MCP registration, while keeping unknown providers or config shapes on manual instructions.

User stories covered: 81-84.

## Acceptance criteria

- Installer previews exact OpenCode skill and MCP config changes before applying them and never silently overwrites provider config.
- Installer applies only recognized safe OpenCode config edits; unknown config shapes and other providers produce fallback/manual instructions.
- Setup stores no app-managed secrets and relies on user environment, config, or provider-native auth.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.2

title: Choose tracker when multiple are detected
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Handle repos that contain more than one supported tracker by asking once instead of guessing and remembering the repo-level choice.

User stories covered: 73, 74.

## Acceptance criteria

- When Beads and `.scratch` are both detected, `/to-qa` asks which tracker to use instead of guessing.
- The selected tracker is remembered per repo and used on later runs.
- The remembered tracker choice can be changed later without losing existing QA sessions.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.8

title: Handle reruns and export import
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Make repeated `/to-qa` runs and portable backups safe by merging new active-session items without losing state, versioning archived reruns, and supporting schema export/import.

User stories covered: 64, 65, 78.

## Acceptance criteria

- Rerunning `/to-qa` for the same active parent merges only new deduped items and preserves existing pass/fail/skip state.
- Rerunning after archive creates a new session version without mutating archived history.
- Schema export/import round trips sessions, items, evidence, history, archive state, and metadata for backup or debugging.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.2
- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.9

title: Archive completed QA sessions
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Let reviewers manually close out QA sessions only when every item is resolved, while keeping archived evidence searchable and local to the app.

User stories covered: 60-63.

## Acceptance criteria

- A session is archive-eligible only when every item is passed, failed-filed, or skipped with a reason.
- Archive is manual and app-only by default, with no tracker mutation.
- Archived sessions remain preserved and searchable with their QA evidence and history.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.3
- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.7

title: Support .scratch tracker sessions and failures
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Add the second supported v1 tracker path so structured `.scratch` markdown can create QA sessions and receive confirmed failure issues without best-effort freeform parsing.

User stories covered: 57, 71, 72.

## Acceptance criteria

- Structured `.scratch` issue files with documented frontmatter/headings can be read as source work for QA session generation.
- Unsupported freeform `.scratch` markdown fails with setup guidance instead of inferred work.
- Confirmed failure issues are written using the documented `.scratch` convention with local screenshot references preserved when needed.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.2
- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.7

title: Draft and confirm Beads failure issues
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Turn a failed QA item into a reviewable Beads bug only after explicit confirmation, preserving traceability and avoiding duplicate tracker spam.

User stories covered: 51-56, 58, 59.

## Acceptance criteria

- MCP drafts a failure issue from scoped QA context and requires explicit confirmation before any Beads mutation.
- Confirmed failures create or update a deduped Beads bug labeled `needs-triage` and `bug`, linked with `discovered-from` to the source child issue when known or parent issue otherwise.
- Tracker errors preserve the failed draft locally and show exact recovery guidance or copyable issue text.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.6

title: Capture failed QA evidence locally
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

When a QA item fails, open a scoped composer that captures actual behavior and screenshots as local evidence tied to that exact QA check.

User stories covered: 42-50.

## Acceptance criteria

- Failing an item opens a scoped composer showing QA instruction, expected result, source evidence, and a required actual-behavior text box.
- Screenshots can be attached by file picker or clipboard paste only for failed QA items.
- Attached screenshots are copied into app storage with metadata and local references are preserved even when tracker upload is unavailable.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.3

title: Add manual items and soft delete restore
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Let reviewers fill coverage gaps during a QA session while keeping generated and manual checks distinct and recoverable.

User stories covered: 32-36.

## Acceptance criteria

- Reviewer can add a lightweight manual QA item to an existing session and it is visibly marked manual.
- Manual items support the same pass, fail entry point, skip, notes, and screenshot-eligible failure flow as generated items.
- Sessions and items can be soft deleted and restored without losing QA evidence or history.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.3

title: Add pass chime settings
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Add satisfying but lightweight pass feedback so completing routine QA checks feels rewarding without adding audio assets or noisy defaults.

User stories covered: 19-21.

## Acceptance criteria

- Passing an item can play a generated Web Audio-style chime without bundling sound files.
- Reviewer can mute the chime and adjust volume from interaction settings.
- Audio settings persist locally and do not affect item state or history semantics.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.3

title: Execute compact QA checklist
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Let a QA reviewer work through an imported session as a dense, keyboard-friendly software QA checklist with reversible item state, preserved provenance, and searchable context.

User stories covered: 3-5, 11-18, 22-26, 30, 31, 37, 40, 41.

## Acceptance criteria

- Checklist rows show status square, source issue badge, title, low-confidence marker when present, and expandable details for steps, expected result, evidence, and notes.
- Reviewer can pass/unpass, skip with reason, edit generated text while preserving original generated text and source evidence, and inspect state history.
- `j/k`, Space, `f`, `s`, `e`, `/`, Enter, and `a` shortcuts work where applicable and are suppressed while typing in text inputs.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.2

title: Create Beads QA session from /to-qa parent issue
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Make the first end-to-end Sandcastle/RALPH QA loop work for Beads: `/to-qa <parent issue>` gathers completed child work, creates human-verifiable QA items through the write-only MCP inbox, imports them into SQLite, and shows the active session with repo and parent context.

User stories covered: 1-10, 27-29, 38, 39, 66-70, 77, 88, 89.

## Acceptance criteria

- `/to-qa <parent issue>` creates QA items only from closed/completed Beads child work and warns about incomplete children.
- A parent with no completed source work fails clearly without creating unrelated QA.
- Validated MCP inbox JSON imports into SQLite and appears as the most recent active session with repo, parent issue, source evidence, warnings, and default title.

## Blocked by

- qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3.1

title: Bootstrap QA To Do app shell
description:
## Parent

qa-to-do-mcp-tool-ea3

## What to build

Establish the local-first QA To Do app baseline so a user can launch an empty Linux desktop app, see how `/to-qa <parent issue>` will populate sessions, and see whether MCP, inbox, and tracker prerequisites are healthy.

User stories covered: 75, 76, 84, 85, 86.

## Acceptance criteria

- Tauri 2, Vite, Svelte 5, TypeScript, and initial test tooling are wired with `npm run typecheck` and `npm run test`.
- Launching the app with no sessions shows a minimalist empty state explaining `/to-qa <parent issue>`.
- The app shows read-only config health for MCP registration, inbox writability, and tracker readiness without storing secrets.

## Blocked by

None - can start immediately

title: PRD: Sandcastle/RALPH QA To Do App
description:
# Sandcastle/RALPH QA To Do App PRD

## Problem Statement

Humans need a fast, reliable quality assurance step after Sandcastle/RALPH creates and merges AI-generated software work. The current workflow can produce completed Beads issues and `RALPH:` commits, but there is no dedicated place for a human to run focused QA checks, record pass/fail outcomes, preserve evidence, or turn failed QA into follow-up tracker issues.

The user wants a standalone, minimalist QA To Do app for the software QA phase. It should not be a generic personal todo app or a full project manager. It should take completed Sandcastle/RALPH work, produce actionable human QA checks, let the user pass items with satisfying feedback, capture failures with notes and screenshots, and create follow-up issues in the repo's tracker after explicit confirmation.

## Solution

Build a Linux-first standalone QA To Do app for Sandcastle/RALPH output. The primary flow is `/to-qa <parent issue>` from an agent-enabled repo. The agent inspects the parent issue, completed child issues, recent Sandcastle/RALPH commits, and any necessary repo context. The agent creates structured QA checks and calls a write-only MCP tool to create an active QA session in the app.

The app owns QA session state locally. It stores sessions, item state, edits, history, screenshots, archive state, and soft-deleted records in SQLite. MCP writes validated inbox JSON for app import instead of directly mutating the database. The app can be closed when MCP receives a session; it imports pending inbox entries when running or on next launch.

V1 is Sandcastle/RALPH-only for the source model. It supports Beads and a documented `.scratch` markdown convention. GitHub Issues support is intentionally out of scope. Broad provider invocation remains useful: OpenCode, Claude, Cursor, Codex, Zed, Pi, and similar tools may invoke `/to-qa` where they support skills, MCP, or terminal fallback, but the input model remains Sandcastle/RALPH.

When a QA item fails, the app opens a scoped failure composer for that item. The user explains what happened and may attach screenshots by file picker or clipboard paste. The app sends scoped context to MCP. MCP drafts the tracker issue, deduplicates by fingerprint, and waits for explicit confirmation before mutating Beads or `.scratch`. Failed drafts are preserved locally if the tracker is unavailable.

## User Stories

1. As a developer using Sandcastle, I want `/to-qa <parent issue>` to create a QA session from completed RALPH work, so that I can verify AI-generated changes before accepting them.
2. As a developer using Sandcastle, I want the QA source model to focus on RALPH output, so that the app matches my actual post-Sandcastle workflow.
3. As a QA reviewer, I want QA to mean software quality assurance, so that generated items are human verification checks rather than generic todos or questions.
4. As a QA reviewer, I want each QA item to verify one independently testable behavior, so that pass/fail decisions are precise.
5. As a QA reviewer, I want generated QA items to include title, steps, expected result, and source evidence, so that I know exactly what to test and why.
6. As a QA reviewer, I want the app to show which repo and parent issue a session came from, so that I do not QA the wrong work.
7. As a QA reviewer, I want the default session title to include repo and parent issue identity, so that sessions are readable across projects.
8. As a QA reviewer, I want the app to support sessions from multiple repos, so that a global tool can be useful across my projects.
9. As a QA reviewer, I want the app to default to my most recent active session, so that I can resume QA quickly.
10. As a QA reviewer, I want active sessions grouped or filterable by repo, so that cross-project work remains organized.
11. As a QA reviewer, I want the UI to be minimalist and monochrome, so that the checklist stays calm and focused.
12. As a QA reviewer, I want the UI to feel polished rather than barebones, so that the tool feels intentional and worth using.
13. As a QA reviewer, I want semantic red only for failure states, so that failure is clear without turning the UI into a colorful dashboard.
14. As a QA reviewer, I want compact checklist rows, so that I can scan a QA session quickly.
15. As a QA reviewer, I want rows to expand or open detail on selection, so that steps, expected results, evidence, and notes are available without clutter.
16. As a QA reviewer, I want each row to show a status square, source issue badge, and short title, so that the checklist is dense but understandable.
17. As a QA reviewer, I want one square control for pass/fail-oriented interaction, so that each QA item has a simple primary affordance.
18. As a QA reviewer, I want single click or Space to mark a QA item passed, so that routine pass actions are fast.
19. As a QA reviewer, I want a satisfying generated ding when I pass an item, so that completion feels rewarding.
20. As a QA reviewer, I want mute and volume controls for the ding, so that I can use the app in quiet environments.
21. As a QA reviewer, I want the pass sound generated in code, so that the app stays lightweight.
22. As a QA reviewer, I want an explicit fail action instead of double-click, so that I do not accidentally mark an item failed.
23. As a keyboard-focused reviewer, I want `j/k` navigation, Space pass/unpass, `f` fail, `s` skip, `e` edit, `/` search, Enter expand, and `a` archive, so that I can work quickly.
24. As a new user, I want a shortcut preview on the main screen, so that I can discover keyboard controls without reading docs.
25. As a QA reviewer, I want hotkeys disabled while typing in text inputs, so that writing notes does not trigger actions.
26. As a QA reviewer, I want search and filters for text, status, source issue, repo, and session, so that large QA sessions remain navigable.
27. As a QA reviewer, I want subtle warning markers on low-confidence generated items, so that I know when source criteria were weak.
28. As a QA reviewer, I want low-confidence warnings not to block checking items off, so that QA can continue even when source issues are imperfect.
29. As a QA reviewer, I want generated items inferred from weak criteria to be marked, so that I understand the limits of the generated QA list.
30. As a QA reviewer, I want to edit generated QA items, so that unclear wording can be fixed quickly.
31. As a QA reviewer, I want original generated text and source evidence preserved after edits, so that provenance is not lost.
32. As a QA reviewer, I want to add lightweight manual QA items to a session, so that gaps in generated coverage can be captured immediately.
33. As a QA reviewer, I want manual items marked as manual, so that generated coverage and human-added coverage remain distinguishable.
34. As a QA reviewer, I want manual items to support the same pass, fail, skip, notes, and screenshot flow, so that ad hoc checks feel first-class.
35. As a QA reviewer, I want to soft delete bad sessions or items, so that generated mistakes can be cleaned up without losing recoverability.
36. As a QA reviewer, I want deleted sessions and items restorable, so that accidental deletion does not destroy QA evidence.
37. As a QA reviewer, I want no QA-specific due dates or priorities in v1, so that the app does not become a project manager.
38. As a QA reviewer, I want source issue priority shown only as read-only metadata when available, so that tracker context is visible without adding scheduling features.
39. As a QA reviewer, I want automated test/build/CI evidence shown as read-only session evidence, so that I can consider it without letting it replace human QA.
40. As a QA reviewer, I want passing a completed item to be reversible, so that human mistakes can be corrected.
41. As a QA reviewer, I want state changes to preserve history, so that QA decisions are auditable.
42. As a QA reviewer, I want failed items to open a scoped composer, so that failure reporting stays tied to the exact QA check.
43. As a QA reviewer, I want the failure composer to show the QA instruction, expected result, source evidence, and a text box for actual behavior, so that I can report failures precisely.
44. As a QA reviewer, I want failure drafting to require my failure note, so that created bugs are not empty or vague.
45. As a QA reviewer, I want screenshots only for failed QA items in v1, so that screenshot evidence stays focused on bug reporting.
46. As a QA reviewer, I want to add screenshots by file picker, so that saved evidence can be attached.
47. As a QA reviewer, I want to paste screenshots from the clipboard, so that failure evidence can be captured quickly.
48. As a QA reviewer, I want the app to copy screenshots into app storage, so that evidence is not lost if original files move.
49. As a QA reviewer, I want screenshots uploaded or embedded when the tracker supports it, so that failure issues carry evidence.
50. As a QA reviewer, I want local screenshot references preserved when upload is unsupported, so that evidence is not silently dropped.
51. As a QA reviewer, I want MCP to draft a failure issue before any tracker mutation, so that I can review what will be filed.
52. As a QA reviewer, I want to explicitly confirm failure issue creation or update, so that the tool does not spam the tracker.
53. As a QA reviewer, I want failure issues deduped by fingerprint, so that repeated failures do not create duplicate tracker bugs.
54. As a QA reviewer, I want existing matching failure issues updated or commented on when appropriate, so that follow-up context stays together.
55. As a QA reviewer, I want failed QA issues labeled `needs-triage` and `bug`, so that they enter the normal triage flow safely.
56. As a QA reviewer, I want Beads failure issues linked with `discovered-from` to the source child or parent issue, so that follow-up defects preserve traceability.
57. As a QA reviewer, I want `.scratch` failure issues written using a documented convention, so that local markdown tracking remains parseable.
58. As a QA reviewer, I want failed drafts saved locally when tracker creation fails, so that failures are not lost.
59. As a QA reviewer, I want tracker errors to show exact recovery guidance or copyable issue text, so that I can file later.
60. As a QA reviewer, I want a session to be archive-eligible only when every item is passed, failed-filed, or skipped with a reason, so that unresolved failures cannot disappear.
61. As a QA reviewer, I want archive to be manual, so that I can review the completed session before closing it.
62. As a QA reviewer, I want archive to be app-only by default, so that tracker state is not mutated by local QA cleanup.
63. As a QA reviewer, I want archived sessions preserved and searchable, so that previous QA evidence remains available.
64. As a QA reviewer, I want rerunning `/to-qa` for the same active parent to merge only new deduped items, so that existing pass/fail state is preserved.
65. As a QA reviewer, I want rerunning `/to-qa` after archive to create a new session version, so that archived history remains immutable.
66. As a QA reviewer, I want `/to-qa <parent issue>` to create items only from completed or closed source work, so that the checklist is executable.
67. As a QA reviewer, I want incomplete child work summarized as a warning or count, so that I know the parent-level QA may be partial.
68. As a QA reviewer, I want an empty parent with no completed work to fail clearly instead of creating unrelated QA, so that mistakes are not hidden.
69. As a Beads user, I want closed child issues under the parent to be the primary Beads source, so that QA follows my Sandcastle issue decomposition.
70. As a Beads user, I want the parent being closed not to imply all children are complete, so that QA scope is accurate.
71. As a `.scratch` user, I want v1 to support only documented markdown/frontmatter issue files, so that parsing is reliable.
72. As a `.scratch` user, I want unsupported freeform markdown to fail with setup guidance, so that the tool does not infer the wrong work.
73. As a user with multiple trackers in one repo, I want the tool to ask which tracker to use, so that it does not guess incorrectly.
74. As a user with multiple trackers in one repo, I want the chosen tracker remembered per repo and changeable later, so that repeated runs are convenient.
75. As a user launching the app before any sessions exist, I want a minimal empty state explaining `/to-qa <parent issue>`, so that the app does not feel broken.
76. As a user launching the app, I want config health indicators for MCP, inbox writability, and tracker readiness, so that setup issues are visible.
77. As a user, I want `/to-qa` session creation to work when the app is closed, so that the agent workflow does not require app launch first.
78. As a user, I want an optional command or deep link to open the app to a created session, so that I can jump into QA when ready.
79. As a user, I want schema export/import, so that sessions can be backed up, debugged, or moved without cloud sync.
80. As a Linux user, I want a `.deb` package, so that the app installs cleanly on Debian/Ubuntu-style systems.
81. As a Linux user, I want an AppImage, so that I can run the app on other Linux distributions without root.
82. As an agent user, I want a global `/to-qa` skill where supported, so that I do not need to copy skill files into each repo.
83. As an agent user, I want providers without native skill support to have MCP/rules/CLI fallback instructions, so that the workflow is still usable.
84. As an installer user, I want preview-then-apply setup, so that provider config is never silently overwritten.
85. As a security-conscious user, I want no app-managed secrets in v1, so that credentials stay in user env/config/provider auth.
86. As an implementation agent, I want `npm run typecheck` and `npm run test` to exist from the start, so that Sandcastle quality gates are real.
87. As an implementation agent, I want Sandcastle's existing Beads-first automation preserved, so that building this product does not disrupt repo workflow.
88. As a maintainer, I want GitHub Issues out of v1, so that implementation stays focused on trackers the user actually uses.
89. As a maintainer, I want the app to be single-user local-first in v1, so that team sync does not block the first useful version.
90. As a maintainer, I want the first success test to be the end-to-end RALPH QA loop, so that v1 proves the real workflow before expanding.

## Implementation Decisions

- V1 is specifically a Sandcastle/RALPH QA To Do app, not a generic QA platform.
- Broad provider support means broad invocation support for `/to-qa` and MCP, while the input model remains Sandcastle/RALPH.
- The visible workflow is skill to MCP to app. CLI support may exist as fallback or internal plumbing, but it is not the primary product story.
- The app owns QA session state, item state, edit history, screenshots, archive state, soft delete state, and settings.
- The tracker remains the source of truth for engineering issues, not for local QA checklist state.
- Supported v1 trackers are Beads and a documented `.scratch` markdown convention.
- GitHub Issues support is out of scope for v1.
- If multiple supported trackers are detected, the user is asked which one to use. The choice is remembered per repo and can be changed later.
- `/to-qa <parent issue>` is the primary generation entry point. Parent inference may be a convenience later but should not replace the explicit path.
- Beads scope comes from the parent issue and completed closed children. Open children may be summarized as warnings but do not generate actionable QA items by default.
- For Beads, completed child work should be discovered through exhaustive parent-scoped listing rather than default-limited child commands.
- A parent with zero completed source work should not create a session by default.
- `.scratch` support requires structured frontmatter or documented headings for ID, title, status, parent, and acceptance notes.
- `.scratch` freeform markdown should fail with setup guidance rather than best-effort parsing.
- The agent may inspect whatever repo/tracker context is necessary, but it should start from parent issue, completed children, recent RALPH commits, changed files, and read deeper only when needed.
- The MCP is write-only from the agent-facing perspective. It does not provide repo/tracker context gathering tools.
- MCP validates finished QA sessions and failure actions, then writes app inbox messages.
- MCP v1 tools are session creation, failure issue drafting, and confirmed failure issue creation/update.
- MCP must not expose pass, fail, edit, archive, or delete operations for QA items in v1.
- MCP must enforce strict schema validation for QA sessions.
- Each QA item requires a title, human-verifiable steps, expected result, source evidence, stable ID, and fingerprint.
- MCP should reject or warn on vague implementation-oriented QA items that are not human-verifiable checks.
- MCP writes validated inbox JSON first. The app imports inbox entries into SQLite.
- The app can be closed when MCP creates a session. Inbox entries are imported on next app launch or while running.
- Processed inbox entries are moved to processed retention with result metadata for debugging and dedupe.
- The app stores durable state in SQLite and uses JSON only as the MCP, import, export, and backup contract.
- A browser dev mode should exist for fast UI iteration with mock/local data. Desktop integration remains in Tauri and MCP flows.
- The app uses Tauri 2, Vite, Svelte 5, TypeScript, SQLite, and JSON schema boundaries.
- Linux is the v1 platform target, with `.deb` and AppImage packaging.
- The global installer previews changes before applying them and must not silently overwrite provider config.
- The app should show minimal onboarding when empty, including the `/to-qa <parent issue>` workflow and config health indicators.
- The app should support multiple repos, grouped or filterable in a simple session list.
- Session titles default to repo plus parent issue identity.
- QA item display is compact with expandable detail.
- The app includes minimal keyboard shortcuts and a shortcut preview on the main screen.
- Pass feedback uses a generated Web Audio-style chime with mute and volume controls.
- The failure composer is scoped to one QA item and is not a general chatbot.
- Failure drafting is handled through MCP from scoped app context.
- Failure context defaults to QA item, expected result, source evidence, repo/tracker metadata, user failure note, and screenshots. MCP re-investigates only if explicitly requested.
- Failure issue creation is draft first, confirm second. Tracker mutation requires explicit confirmation.
- Failure issues are deduped by stable fingerprint before creation.
- Beads failure bugs use `discovered-from` against the source child issue when known, otherwise the parent issue.
- Failure issues default to type `bug` and labels `needs-triage` and `bug`.
- Screenshots are supported in v1 only for failed QA items.
- Screenshot input uses file picker and clipboard paste, not built-in screen capture.
- Screenshots are copied into app storage and uploaded or embedded in tracker issues only when the tracker adapter supports it.
- Generated prompts and private reasoning are not stored by default. Store evidence, generated outputs, warnings, model/provider metadata, and timestamps.
- QA items can be edited with original generated text and source evidence preserved.
- Manual QA items can be added to existing sessions and are clearly marked manual.
- Pass/fail/skip states are reversible with history.
- Sessions become archive-eligible only when all items are terminal: passed, failed-filed, or skipped with a reason.
- Archive is manual and app-only by default.
- Soft delete is supported for cleanup and restore.
- Dates, due dates, and QA-specific priorities are not included in v1.
- App-triggered rerun/generation is not included in v1. The app may show the rerun command.
- The current Sandcastle workflow for this repo remains Beads-first. Product tracker abstraction is separate from implementation automation.

### Deep Modules To Build

- QA Session Domain: owns session and item state transitions, archive eligibility, rerun merge behavior, edit history, soft delete, and terminal-state rules behind a small command interface.
- Session Schema Validator: owns MCP/import/export schema validation, QA item quality validation, versioning, and actionable error reporting.
- MCP Inbox Writer: owns write-only tool validation, atomic inbox entry creation, idempotency, and correlation IDs.
- Inbox Importer: owns idempotent import from processed inbox artifacts into app storage, duplicate detection, and import error retention.
- Storage Repository: owns SQLite persistence for sessions, items, history, screenshots, drafts, settings, archive, and export/import.
- Tracker Adapter Layer: owns Beads and `.scratch` detection, source issue reading, failure issue drafting, failure issue creation/update, dedupe search, and capability reporting.
- Failure Issue Flow: owns scoped failure draft generation, dedupe fingerprinting, confirm-then-mutate rules, local failed-draft fallback, and tracker result reconciliation.
- Screenshot Store: owns picker/paste imports, validation, local copy, metadata, linking to failed items, and tracker attachment/reference behavior.
- Interaction Settings: owns pass chime configuration, mute/volume, hotkey policy, and reduced-motion/reduced-audio behavior where detectable.
- App Shell/UI: owns minimalist session list, checklist view, item detail, failure composer, archive prompts, search/filter, empty state, and shortcut preview.
- Installer/Provider Setup: owns global skill/MCP setup, preview-then-apply configuration, provider capability reporting, optional app-open command, and fallback instructions.

## Testing Decisions

- This QA To Do project should include quality gates from the start.
- Sandcastle's existing expected quality gates must remain `npm run typecheck` and `npm run test`.
- Adding tests must not disrupt the current Sandcastle Beads-first workflow.
- Tests should verify external behavior, not private helpers or implementation details.
- Schema tests should verify valid QA session payloads, missing required fields, duplicate fingerprints, unsupported schema versions, and vague non-human-verifiable QA items.
- Domain tests should verify item transitions, reversibility, edit history, terminal states, archive eligibility, and invalid transition rejection.
- Rerun tests should verify active-session merge behavior preserves existing pass/fail state and archived-session reruns create new versions.
- MCP tests should verify strict validation, atomic inbox write behavior, idempotency, app-closed session creation, rejection of invalid sessions, and lack of pass/fail mutation tools.
- Importer tests should verify valid imports, invalid entry quarantine, processed retention, duplicate import safety, and preserving generated text, warnings, evidence, timestamps, and metadata.
- Storage tests should verify persisted sessions, item history, failure drafts, tracker issue links, screenshots metadata, archive state, soft delete, and export/import round trips.
- Tracker adapter tests should verify Beads detection, parent closed child discovery, Beads `discovered-from` failure relation, default labels, `.scratch` convention parsing, `.scratch` failure issue writing, and unsupported tracker behavior.
- Failure flow tests should verify scoped context, required user failure note, draft-first behavior, explicit confirmation before tracker mutation, dedupe fingerprint handling, local failed-draft fallback, and transition to failed-filed on success.
- Screenshot tests should verify picker/paste metadata, failure-only attachment rules, local copy behavior, tracker capability handling, and local path/reference fallback.
- Keyboard/UI behavior tests should verify visible state changes for navigation, pass, fail, skip, edit, search, expand, archive, and hotkey suppression while typing.
- Archive tests should verify only passed, failed-filed, and skipped-with-reason states are terminal.
- Packaging/setup tests should verify installer dry-run output, no silent config overwrite, provider capability detection, and safe fallback instructions.
- Prior art in this repo is minimal: current package metadata lacks app code and lacks `test`/`typecheck` scripts. Initial implementation should establish the project testing pattern.

## Out of Scope

- GitHub Issues support in v1.
- GitLab, Jira, Linear, or other remote tracker adapters in v1.
- Generic QA checklist app behavior beyond Sandcastle/RALPH v1.
- Non-Sandcastle provider-specific source formats in v1.
- Team sync, shared accounts, shared databases, or real-time collaboration.
- Cloud sync.
- Built-in screen capture.
- General file attachments beyond failed-item screenshots.
- App-managed secrets, keychain integration, or built-in credential vault in v1.
- Dates, due dates, priorities, scheduling, or productivity planning.
- Dashboards, analytics, reporting views, or productivity stats.
- Storing full prompts, chain-of-thought, or private model reasoning by default.
- Direct SQLite writes from MCP.
- MCP read/query APIs for app state.
- Agent-driven pass/fail/edit/archive/delete mutations.
- Automatic tracker mutation without explicit confirmation.
- App-triggered QA generation or rerun buttons in v1.
- Replacing or generalizing this repo's Sandcastle Beads-first implementation automation.
- macOS or Windows packaging in v1.

## Further Notes

- QA means quality assurance in the software development lifecycle.
- The first useful version succeeds when, after a Sandcastle/RALPH run, `/to-qa <parent issue>` creates an active QA session; the user can pass checks with a ding; the user can fail a check with a note and screenshot; the user can confirm a Beads or `.scratch` failure issue; and the completed session can be manually archived.
- Beads should prove the end-to-end core loop first. `.scratch` proves adapter breadth after the core loop is stable.
- The app should not feel like a tracker replacement. It is a local QA execution surface that can round-trip failures into the tracker.
- Low-quality source issues should not block all QA generation. The agent can infer useful checks from issues, commits, and code, but low-confidence items should be marked subtly.
- The installer must be conservative. Preview changes, merge safely where possible, and print manual instructions rather than overwriting provider configuration.
- No app secrets are stored in v1. Credentials live in user environment/config or provider-native auth, and the app may show config health without collecting keys.
- The PRD intentionally removes GitHub Issues because the user does not use it.

comments.author: user
comments.text:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Keep the Sandcastle/RALPH QA To Do App PRD as human-owned product context.

**Current behavior:**
This issue defines the product problem, solution, user stories, implementation decisions, testing decisions, and out-of-scope boundaries. It is the parent/source context for child implementation issues rather than one independently grabbable implementation slice.

**Desired behavior:**
The parent PRD should remain the human-owned source of product direction while child issues carry implementation briefs for agents. Scope, sequencing, and v1 boundary changes should be made intentionally by the maintainer before being decomposed into implementation work.

**Key interfaces:**
- Product scope and implementation decisions.
- Child issue dependency graph.
- Triage labels and readiness state.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] The issue remains available as the source context for child implementation work.
- [ ] Implementation happens through child issues rather than directly through this parent PRD.
- [ ] Scope or v1 boundary changes are reviewed by the maintainer before child work changes.

**Why this needs human implementation:**
This issue represents product direction and work decomposition, not a single code change. Agents can work on the child issues, but the maintainer should own changes to product scope and acceptance boundaries.

**Out of scope:**
- Implementing all child slices inside this parent issue.
- Closing the parent before child implementation set is accepted.
- Expanding v1 beyond the documented out-of-scope boundaries.
